sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 96, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Center bar restored. Player at [52-54, 32-35], W box at [55-59, 32-35]. Level not complete. Boxes still in wrong positions (2 c + 1 W vs initial 3 c).
- Plan: Try pressing SPACE to deactivate W box, then check if level completes with all boxes as c.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 95 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After pressing SPACE:
- W box at [55-59, 32-35] became G box at [56-59, 32-35] (shrank from 16 px to 12 px, rows 56-59 instead of 55-59)
- Center bar still at [28-31, 28-39] with child [4] (still restored)
- Level not complete
So pressing SPACE changed the W box to G (and it shrank). The G box is now at [56-59, 32-35] with 12 px (3x4 object, same as c boxes).
Let me think about what I know:
1. SPACE toggles box colors: c → W → G → c (or similar cycle)
2. The W box follows the player
3. The center bar shrinks when boxes are activated and restores when the player moves away
Wait, let me reconsider. The center bar was restored when I moved the player DOWN. But the center bar is still restored even after pressing SPACE. So the center bar state is independent of the box activation state.
Let me think about what the goal might be. The initial state had:
- 3 c boxes at specific positions
- 1 b bar with g interior
- Player at [49-51, 32-35]
The current state has:
- 2 c boxes + 1 G box at different positions
- 1 b bar with g interior (restored)
- Player at [52-54, 32-35]
The boxes are in different positions. The initial c boxes were at [24-27, 44-47], [28-31, 16-19], [36-39, 32-35]. The current boxes are at [28-31, 16-19], [32-35, 20-23], [56-59, 32-35].
Wait, the G box at [56-59, 32-35] is at a different position than the initial c box at [36-39, 32-35]. The G box is 17 rows below the initial c box.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me think about the game differently.
Maybe the goal is not to restore the initial state, but to achieve a specific configuration. Let me think about what configuration would make sense.
Actually, let me reconsider the game mechanics. The W box follows the player. When the player moves, the W box moves to follow. The center bar moves in the opposite direction.
Wait, but the center bar is now restored and hasn't moved even after the player moved. Let me check if the center bar moves when the player moves.
Actually, looking at the current state, the center bar is at [28-31, 28-39], which is the same as the initial state. The player is at [52-54, 32-35], which is different from the initial position [49-51, 32-35].
Maybe the center bar only moves when the player is in a certain row range. Let me think...
Actually, I think the center bar is "attached" to the player in some way. When the player is close to the center bar (same row range), the center bar moves. When the player is far from the center bar, the center bar stays in place.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the initial state and think about what the goal might be.
Initial state:
- Player at [49-51, 32-35]
- c box at [36-39, 32-35] (above player)
- c box at [28-31, 16-19] (left)
- c box at [24-27, 44-47] (top-right)
- Center bar at [28-31, 28-39] with gray interior
The player was below a c box. The c box was at [36-39, 32-35], and the player was at [49-51, 32-35]. So the c box was 9 rows above the player.
Wait, that's a big gap. Let me recalculate. The c box was at rows 36-39, and the player was at rows 49-51. The gap is 49 - 39 = 10 rows.
Hmm, that's a lot of space between the c box and the player. Maybe there was something else in between.
Actually, let me reconsider. The initial state had the player at [49-51, 32-35], and the c box at [36-39, 32-35]. The c box was directly above the player, with a gap of 10 rows.
In the current state, the player is at [52-54, 32-35], and the G box is at [56-59, 32-35]. The G box is directly below the player, with a gap of 2 rows.
So the G box follows the player, but it's now below the player instead of above. This is different from the initial state where the c box was above the player.
Wait, but the initial state had a c box at [36-39, 32-35], which was above the player at [49-51, 32-35]. The c box was not following the player; it was a fixed object.
But in the current state, the G box at [56-59, 32-35] is following the player at [52-54, 32-35]. The G box is the "active" box that follows the player.
So the game mechanics are:
1. There are 3 fixed c boxes in the initial state.
2. When the player activates a c box (by pressing SPACE while adjacent), it becomes W and follows the player.
3. When the player activates another c box, the previous W box becomes G (or c) and stays in place.
4. The center bar shrinks when boxes are activated and restores when the player moves away.
Wait, but the center bar is still restored even though the player is at [52-54, 32-35], which is close to the initial position. Let me think...
Actually, maybe the center bar state depends on the player's row position. When the player is in rows 28-35 (same as the center bar), the center bar moves. When the player is in rows 48-55 (below the center bar), the center bar stays in place.
Let me test this hypothesis by moving the player UP into the center bar row range.
Actually, I think I need to step back and think about what the goal might be. The initial state had a specific configuration, and the current state is different. The goal might be to achieve a specific configuration, not necessarily the initial one.
Let me think about what configuration would make sense:
1. All 3 boxes activated (W) - but that's impossible since only one box can be W at a time.
2. All 3 boxes in specific positions - but the boxes move when activated.
3. Center bar in a specific state - the center bar is already restored.
4. Player in a specific position - the player is close to the initial position.
Actually, maybe the goal is to have the center bar fully restored AND all boxes in their initial positions. But the boxes have moved, so I need to move them back.
Wait, but the boxes are fixed objects. They don't move unless activated. The initial c boxes were at [24-27, 44-47], [28-31, 16-19], [36-39, 32-35]. The current c boxes are at [28-31, 16-19] and [32-35, 20-23]. The G box is at [56-59, 32-35].
The c box at [28-31, 16-19] is the same as the initial c box at [28-31, 16-19]. So this box hasn't moved.
The c box at [32-35, 20-23] is different from any initial c box position.
The G box at [56-59, 32-35] is different from any initial c box position.
So 2 out of 3 boxes have moved from their initial positions. This suggests that the boxes move when activated, and they don't return to their initial positions.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me think about the game differently.
Maybe the goal is not related to the box positions at all. Maybe the goal is to have the center bar in a specific state, or to have the player in a specific position.
Let me try pressing SPACE again to see if the G box becomes c, and if the level completes.

sections.content:

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Press SPACE to cycle G→c
result = action(['SPACE'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After SPACE:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'G', 'W', 'N', 'b', 'g'] and n['pixels'] >= 8:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], px={n['pixels']}, children={n['children']}")
print(f"Level completed: {result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
